text.title: Vendor Evaluation and Antipatterns
text.summary: Weighted scoring for vendor selection, sensitivity analysis requirement, and common evaluation antipatterns.

section.title: Weighted Scoring

rule.statement: Define evaluation criteria and weights before receiving proposals; changing weights after seeing proposals anchors the result.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc weight adjustment is a well-documented anchoring bias — it reverse-engineers justification for a predetermined winner.

rule.statement: Run sensitivity analysis: shift each top weight by ±5% and verify the winner does not flip. If it does, the decision margin is too small to be reliable without deeper due diligence.
rule.rationale: A margin below 5% in a weighted matrix is statistical noise, not a defensible decision.

section.reference: Weighted scorer with automatic sensitivity check

antipattern.description: Lowest-price wins: selecting the cheapest vendor without weighting quality, experience, and risk.
antipattern.reason: Price is the most legible criterion but the weakest predictor of delivery success. Low-price vendors frequently compensate by minimising scope or requesting change orders.

antipattern.description: Acceptance criteria written as subjective goals ("high quality code", "good performance").
antipattern.reason: Subjective criteria cannot be evaluated at acceptance time, creating disputes. Require testable thresholds: defect density, response time, test coverage percentage.

antipattern.description: Evaluation matrix weighted at 5–10% for references, then not interviewing references in a structured way.
antipattern.reason: References are positive-bias by selection; without a rubric the criterion adds no information and can be gamed.

antipattern.description: Multi-supplier / prime + sub arrangements treated as a single-vendor procurement.
antipattern.reason: Partner ecosystems require separate SOWs, liability chains, and IP ownership clauses per party. Combining them obscures accountability.

section.title: Legal Clauses the Agent Cannot Generate

rule.statement: Always flag legal review required on: limitation of liability cap, indemnification scope, IP ownership, data residency, sub-processor terms, jurisdiction, and termination for convenience.
rule.rationale: LLMs default to US-style clauses and miss EU GDPR processor terms, HIPAA BAAs, and jurisdiction-specific UWG/MAC provisions. Legal review is non-negotiable.
